findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is caudal protrusion of peg-shaped cerebellar tonsils greater than 5 mm below the level of the foramen magnum, which is defined by the basion-opisthion line [1, 2]. This downward herniation is frequently accompanied by a small posterior fossa, while the fourth ventricle and cerebellar vermis remain in a relatively normal anatomical position [2, 3].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the presence of a syrinx or syringohydromyelia within the spinal cord, appearing isointense to cerebrospinal fluid on all pulse sequences [3, 4]. Additionally, there may be associated osseous abnormalities of the skull base and cervical spine, such as basilar invagination or Klippel-Feil syndrome [2, 5].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a lumbar myelomeningocele or supratentorial anomalies such as a beaked tectum or enlarged massa intermedia [2, 6]. Furthermore, there is no discrete posterior fossa space-occupying lesion exerting local mass effect [6, 7].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the isolated downward displacement of peg-like cerebellar tonsils without an associated posterior fossa mass or complex hindbrain dysgenesis [3, 7].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Chiari I malformation, given the isolated >5 mm tonsillar ectopia and associated syringohydromyelia [2]. This congenital hindbrain dysgenesis is often diagnosed in young adulthood when patients present with symptoms related to cervicomedullary compression or the syrinx itself [3].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Arnold–Chiari malformation, however this typically involves caudal displacement of the cerebellar vermis, medulla, and fourth ventricle rather than just the tonsils [2, 8]. Furthermore, Arnold-Chiari (Chiari II) malformation is almost always associated with a neural tube closure defect like a myelomeningocele and specific intracranial findings like a beaked tectum, which are absent here [2, 8].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Ependymoma, given that it, along with other posterior fossa masses, can cause secondary downward herniation of the cerebellar tonsils due to mass effect [7]. Missing an underlying neoplasm would result in completely inappropriate treatment, though ependymomas typically present as a distinct mass filling the fourth ventricle rather than isolated tonsillar ectopia [9].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the entire neuroaxis to assess the degree of cervicomedullary compression and identify any associated syringohydromyelia [3, 8].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to monitor the size of any associated syrinx cavity and determine if the degree of tonsillar herniation or brainstem compression is worsening over time [3, 4].
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the whole spine if only a brain or cervical spine MRI has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining the full extent of a syrinx and confidently excluding any spinal dysraphism or occult lower spinal masses [4, 8].
management.mriSequences: I would specify sagittal and axial T1 and T2-weighted MRI sequences to accurately measure the extent of tonsillar herniation below the basion-opisthion line, assess the internal architecture of the spinal cord, and evaluate the effacement of the anterior subarachnoid space [1, 4].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neurosurgery MDT. My report would clearly document the exact distance the cerebellar tonsils extend below the foramen magnum, the presence and extent of any syringohydromyelia, and the degree of CSF space effacement at the craniovertebral junction to guide consideration for surgical decompression [1, 4, 8].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is caudal protrusion of peg-shaped cerebellar tonsils greater than 5 mm below the level of the foramen magnum, which is defined by the basion-opisthion line [1, 2]. This downward herniation is frequently accompanied by a small posterior fossa, while the fourth ventricle and cerebellar vermis remain in a relatively normal anatomical position [2, 3]. Supporting features include the presence of a syrinx or syringohydromyelia within the spinal cord, appearing isointense to cerebrospinal fluid on all pulse sequences [3, 4]. Additionally, there may be associated osseous abnormalities of the skull base and cervical spine, such as basilar invagination or Klippel-Feil syndrome [2, 5]. Importantly, there is no evidence of a lumbar myelomeningocele or supratentorial anomalies such as a beaked tectum or enlarged massa intermedia [2, 6]. Furthermore, there is no discrete posterior fossa space-occupying lesion exerting local mass effect [6, 7]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the isolated downward displacement of peg-like cerebellar tonsils without an associated posterior fossa mass or complex hindbrain dysgenesis [3, 7].

The appearances are most in keeping with Chiari I malformation, given the isolated >5 mm tonsillar ectopia and associated syringohydromyelia [2]. This congenital hindbrain dysgenesis is often diagnosed in young adulthood when patients present with symptoms related to cervicomedullary compression or the syrinx itself [3]. The principle differential would be Arnold–Chiari malformation, however this typically involves caudal displacement of the cerebellar vermis, medulla, and fourth ventricle rather than just the tonsils [2, 8]. Furthermore, Arnold-Chiari (Chiari II) malformation is almost always associated with a neural tube closure defect like a myelomeningocele and specific intracranial findings like a beaked tectum, which are absent here [2, 8]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Ependymoma, given that it, along with other posterior fossa masses, can cause secondary downward herniation of the cerebellar tonsils due to mass effect [7]. Missing an underlying neoplasm would result in completely inappropriate treatment, though ependymomas typically present as a distinct mass filling the fourth ventricle rather than isolated tonsillar ectopia [9].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate the entire neuroaxis to assess the degree of cervicomedullary compression and identify any associated syringohydromyelia [3, 8]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to monitor the size of any associated syrinx cavity and determine if the degree of tonsillar herniation or brainstem compression is worsening over time [3, 4]. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the whole spine if only a brain or cervical spine MRI has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining the full extent of a syrinx and confidently excluding any spinal dysraphism or occult lower spinal masses [4, 8]. I would specify sagittal and axial T1 and T2-weighted MRI sequences to accurately measure the extent of tonsillar herniation below the basion-opisthion line, assess the internal architecture of the spinal cord, and evaluate the effacement of the anterior subarachnoid space [1, 4]. This should go to the Neurosurgery MDT. My report would clearly document the exact distance the cerebellar tonsils extend below the foramen magnum, the presence and extent of any syringohydromyelia, and the degree of CSF space effacement at the craniovertebral junction to guide consideration for surgical decompression [1, 4, 8].
